title: Schmidt v. City of Norfolk Brief: Automated License Plate Readers Commit Fourth Amendment Searches - Cato Institute
content: Sign up to have blog posts delivered straight to your inbox! Norfolk, Virginia, has deployed nearly 200 automated license plate readers (ALPRs) across the city, capturing every passing vehicle’s location, time, and identifying details—and storing that data for weeks. Now, two residents are suing, arguing that this sweeping, warrantless tracking of their daily movements amounts to unconstitutional surveillance under the Fourth Amendment. ALPRs “are high-speed, computer-controlled camera systems” that capture all license plate numbers, along with the location, date, and time. Norfolk has deployed advanced ALPRs from the technology company Flock that use artificial intelligence to record the make, type, color, and distinctive features of a vehicle (like bumper stickers) to create a trackable “Vehicle Fingerprint.” They are capable of revealing how fast and in what direction a person traveled and can even reconstruct a vehicle’s historical routes over time. In just a matter of keystrokes, officers can chart a person’s full movement across a city over a specific timeframe, with almost no restrictions or oversight. The ALPRs installed by the City of Norfolk automatically record every vehicle that passes by and archive that information for 21 days. The Appellants in this case—Lee Schmidt and Crystal Arrington—are Norfolk residents whose cars are captured by the city’s ALPRs, sometimes several times per day. They sued, alleging that the city’s warrantless collection of their movements throughout the city—and retention of those records for future review and inspection—violates the Fourth Amendment. The district court dismissed their complaint, holding that the ALPRs do not violate the Fourth Amendment because they do not track “the whole” of their movements. Although the city’s ALPRs are stationary and do not persistently track people the way a cell phone or a drone can, the public nature of a roadway is not a blank check for government surveillance. The Supreme Court once held that a person has no expectation of privacy in “movements from one place to another,” but it has since refined that analysis. As technology has advanced, the Court has recognized that even data collected in public spaces can offer an intimate window into a person’s life—revealing where they travel, how often, and for how long—and that “more sophisticated” surveillance methods can violate the Fourth Amendment. Norfolk’s ALPR network qualifies as such a system. Cato has filed an amicus brief to highlight two points. First, the Fourth Amendment was designed to eliminate indiscriminate searches and the threat of “general warrants” that allowed British officials to rummage through Americans’ homes and papers. Today’s technology poses an analogous threat: with no probable cause, government officials can now collect and store intimate details of a person’s life—including financial information and location information—for perusal at the click of a button. Without judicial oversight, tools like ALPRs give police the discretionary power to conduct suspicionless searches that the Framers sought to prohibit. Second, the district court’s rule—accepting all government tracking short of the very most exhaustive kind—would invite the warrantless deployment of many technologies to surveil the public. Modern surveillance tools such as ALPRs, military-grade drones, financial “audit trails,” and other technologies and sensors let the government monitor millions of people with ease, tracking movements, detecting faces, recording digital conversations—then storing everything for later review. Left unchecked, such tools would render the Fourth Amendment’s warrant requirement a dead letter. Cato has asked the Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals to reverse the decision and to foreclose a surveillance apparatus that silently records the daily movements of every Norfolk resident who ventures onto a public street. Every weekday morning: independent analysis grounded in individual liberty, limited government, free markets, and peace.

title: State Senate 46th District Republican primary: Camera Bartolotta v. Al Buchtan - 90.5 WESA
content: What's at stake: Perhaps the most contentious primary in southwestern Pennsylvania is unfolding in state Senate District 46, where three-term incumbent Republican Camera Bartolotta faces a spirited primary challenge from local businessman Al Buchtan. Bartolotta beat Democrat Timothy Solobay for the seat in 2014, becoming the first woman to represent a district that encompasses all of Greene and Washington counties and a sliver of Beaver. But she’s facing a challenge from her right this time, as Buchtan says he's fighting an insufficiently conservative party establishment. Washington County’s Republican Committee approved a resolution expressing “no confidence” in Bartolotta, which the state Republican Party called “null and void,” countering it with a series of mailers targeting Buchtan for votes he made while on the Carmichaels Area School District board of directors. Allies of Buchtan, who also faced an effort to remove him from the ballot due to questions regarding his residency, have targeted Bartolotta with mailers of their own. The winner, or at least the survivor, of the GOP primary will face Democrat Evan Snyder in the fall. Pennsylvania’s 50 state senators each represent a district of roughly 260,000 voters. Along with the state House, the Senate reviews, revises, and ultimately passes the state budget and the tax rates on which it is based. The body also drafts and passes legislation concerning everything from election and environmental rules to firearms laws, reproductive rights, and criminal penalties. Senators are also individually appointed to committees that specialize in various policy areas and have the power to reshape, stymie, or advance legislation. The Senate also has the power to confirm or deny many gubernatorial nominations to state commissions and other offices. Republicans have maintained solid control of the upper chamber for more than 30 years. State senators serve four-year terms. Their salary is adjusted for inflation each year; they will be paid $113,591.40 in 2026. Before she was elected in 2014, Bartolotta founded and ran a Mon Valley oil-change business. In Harrisburg, she says she’s voted to reduce taxes and promote the state’s energy independence, while pushing for protections for survivors of domestic violence. She has drawn controversy by pushing back on those who criticize a recent influx of Haitian immigrants to Charleroi: Bartolotta credits them for contributing to the local economy while escaping harrowing conditions in Haiti. Bartolotta has sponsored bills to prevent transgender female students from participating in boys’ sports, and to fast-track data center permits if developers agree to environmental standards. As a ranking member of the Labor and Industry committee, she can advance or stymie bills to increase the state’s minimum wage, among other workforce changes. Education: B.S., business administration and psychology, St. Mary’s College of California Related experience: State senator, 2015-present; currently in Senate GOP leadership as majority caucus secretary, and vice chair of the Labor and Industry committee; 31 years as a small business owner; serves on several community boards Supporters/endorsements: U.S. Sen. Dave McCormick; State Treasurer and GOP gubernatorial candidate Stacy Garrity; State troopers and corrections officers groups; pro-life and Second Amendment political action committees; the National Federation of Independent Business, as well as the state Laborers’ District Council and several labor union locals. Total raised: $233,756.23, plus $73,053.31 from prior years.Total spent: $138,494.16 What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? I am currently advancing legislation to alleviate the property tax burden on seniors and veterans. I have active legislation to reduce permitting wait times for major capital investments and job creation. I am also introducing legislation to require AEDs on electric utility worksites to better protect Pennsylvania transmission lineworkers. In addition, I continue to lead several bills that would expand and improve health care access for Pennsylvanians. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? Enacting meaningful permit reform would go a long way toward encouraging new development. Like President Trump, I support limiting venture capital groups from purchasing housing stock and converting it into overpriced rental properties. Additionally, reducing property taxes would further encourage more individuals and families to purchase homes. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? A student’s quality of education should not depend on their ZIP code. Simply throwing more money at the problem will not fix it; we must demand accountability and measurable results. I support establishing statewide purchasing power for textbooks, strengthening structured literacy initiatives, and expanding partnerships with local industry. We also need a fair funding formula that truly is equitable, along with robust apprenticeship programs to better prepare students for the workforce. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? Data centers are a key part of our economic future, and we must ensure Pennsylvania is at the forefront of this new wave of technology while being strategic and responsible. Pennsylvania is uniquely positioned because we have many brownfields from legacy mining and industrial use; these sites should receive priority for data center development, with full consideration of community concerns. Our number one industry is agriculture, and I am committed to protecting valuable farmland from unnecessary conversion. My preference is that hyperscale AI data centers provide their own power generation wherever feasible, so we can meet their significant energy needs while also strengthening overall grid capacity. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? I’ve found in my conversations with voters that the intense polarization we see in social media echo chambers doesn’t reflect where most people really are. Elected officials must remember that public service depends on earning and keeping the public’s trust. We have more in common than what divides us, and we must stay focused on an agenda that strengthens affordability, safeguards our communities, and builds broad-based prosperity. Buchtan founded a construction business based in Greene County and says he’s made political connections with several “patriot groups” in the region, mainly based in Washington County. Buchtan touts his background as a local entrepreneur, union member and former school board director, and his campaign says his priorities include supporting term limits for Harrisburg lawmakers and encouraging students to start trade apprenticeships before graduating high school. Buchtan’s stances include backing Second Amendment protections, reducing state spending and limiting government regulation. Further reading: Pa. Supreme Court says Buchtan can remain on GOP ballot in challenge to Bartolotta (Chris Potter & Tom Riese, WESA) Place of residence: Buchtan owns a home in Greene County and rents another in Washington County. He has listed the rental property as his home in election papers, but his foes accused him of trying to mislead voters into thinking he lived in the more populous part of the district. Courts refused to remove him from the ballot but said he must identify Greene as his home county. Buchtan says he is “a lifelong resident of the 46th District, which includes Washington, Greene and parts of Beaver Counties.” Education: Four-year trade apprenticeship; diploma, Carmichaels Area High School Current occupation: Small businessman and entrepreneur Related experience: Owns a successful small business and served as a school board director at Carmichaels Area School District, both of which he says gave him budget-management experience. Supporters/endorsements: MAGA Conservatives of Pennsylvania PAC Total raised: $50,345, including $10,000 Buchtan loaned to his campaign.Total spent: $33,601 What concerns does your district have that haven't been met by action in Harrisburg, and what would you do about them if elected? Affordability. High taxes, fees and spending have caused everything to go up, and when you don’t get your fiscal house in order, everything else suffers. We need to fix Harrisburg’s addiction to spending. The Pew Charitable Trusts says that Pennsylvania ranked 44th among states in the rate of new housing creation — and that a lack of new homes is a key reason housing is hard to afford. What policies would you support to encourage more housing, and to make housing more affordable? Streamline the permitting process. Make it easier to get needed permits to build houses without too much red tape. Need common-sense practices back in the housing process so permitting doesn’t become such a burden that nobody wants to build. Recent years have seen an increase in the amount of money the state spends on public education after courts ruled that school funding varies so widely as to be unconstitutional. What impact do you think that spending has had in schools within your district, and what changes in education policy, if any, would you seek if elected? Money should always follow the students, not systems. If we continue to fund systems without accountability, then it will be a black hole for taxpayers, and your property taxes will never stop being hiked. And children will never be educated properly because the system prioritizes feeding the school bureaucracy with money rather than learning. That can’t be the priority. The students’ needs come first. What concerns, if any, are you hearing about data centers or artificial intelligence in your district, and what kind of regulation, if any, do you think the state should impose on their development? We need to be careful with this and understand the full impact before we go headfirst into allowing unchecked growth. We must protect our way of life, the communities that could be affected and make sure we don’t drive up utility costs of homeowners. So, we must do our due diligence before determining what impact these data centers will have and where they can be built. Polling and voter registration statistics suggest that in recent years, there has been rising discontent with both major political parties. Why do you think that is, and what do you think your party can do at the state level to address it? It’s because they don’t see a difference in the parties, and you can’t blame them. We have the uni party in Harrisburg, and most of the politicians of both parties are in bed with the lobbyist class that only looks out for the establishment. This needs to stop, and I plan to introduce reforms to stop it. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: US House 12th District Democratic Primary: Summer Lee v. Will Parker - 90.5 WESA
content: What's at stake: Before she was elected to Congress in 2022 and since then, Summer Lee made a name for herself as a leading progressive voice. In Washington, she’s vocally denounced the Trump administration, but she’s also sought to push the political issue on issues such as economic and environmental justice, while questioning American policy abroad as well. Her challenger, Will Parker, previously ran for the seat in 2022 and is known for his campaigns in a number of previous races, including a brief run for mayor of Pittsburgh last year. The winner of the primary is all but certain to face Republican James Hayes, who ran in 2024 and lost to Lee by nearly 13 points. The 12th District is a Democratic stronghold that includes the city of Pittsburgh and a broad swath of outlying areas to the east and south in both Allegheny and Westmoreland counties. Members of the U.S. House draft, debate, and vote on legislation, including the federal budget. They also sit on committees with jurisdiction over specific policy areas. The House also initiates efforts to impeach federal officials, though the Senate must confirm that judgment before the official can be removed. House members serve two-year terms and earn $174,000 per year. Here's who’s on the ballot in Western Pa. for Congress, state legislature & more (Chris Potter, WESA) Pa. politicos seek to knock foes off ballot before May 19 primary (Chris Potter, Tom Riese, WESA) Lee has been among the region’s most prominent progressive leaders since she first ran for state House in 2018. In 2022, she replaced the retiring U.S. Rep. Mike Doyle to become the first Black woman to represent Pennsylvania in Congress. From her seat on the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, she has been a thorn in the side of the Trump administration, demanding the release of investigative files related to Jeffrey Epstein and filing articles of impeachment against Pam Bondi, Trump’s outgoing attorney general. Lee’s criticism of Israel, and the war in Gaza particularly, has drawn criticism from some quarters. But while it was a prominent issue in 2022 and 2024, discussion of the issue has been more muted so far this year. Further reading: At Hill District town hall, Lee argues Democrats should not shy from 'activism and organizing' (Julia Maruca, WESA) In town hall, Summer Lee says democracy could fail, will require sacrifice to save (Rachel McDevitt, WESA) Education: Graduate of Woodland Hills High School (2005); B.A. in journalism with minors in international studies and French, Pennsylvania State University (2009); J.D., the Howard University School of Law (2015) Current occupation: U.S. Representative for Pennsylvania's 12th Congressional District Related experience: Member of Congress (2023-Present), Member of the Pennsylvania State Assembly (2019-2023) Supporters/endorsements: Allegheny County Labor Council, Allegheny County Democratic Committee, Steel City Stonewall Democrats, Young Democrats of Allegheny County, Congressional Black Caucus PAC, Congressional Progressive Caucus PAC, APIPA, Justice Democrats, SEIU Healthcare, 32BJ SEIU, Working Families Party Total raised: $1,224,312.49Total spent: $508,820.73 What is the most important thing the federal government could do for your district, and how would you make it happen if you were elected to Congress? The most important thing the federal government can do is start working for people instead of corporations and billionaire donors. Too many families feel like the rules are rigged because too often they are. When special interests call the shots, our communities pay the price in higher costs, underfunded schools, and broken trust. In Congress, I’ll keep fighting to hold corporate power accountable, get money out of politics, protect our democracy, and make sure government delivers real results for working people. Our communities deserve leadership rooted in service, not personal greed. Polling consistently shows many voters are worried about the cost of living, especially when it comes to essentials such as housing and food. How do you think Congress should address these concerns? Congress should lower costs where families feel it most: housing, food, health care, child care, and utilities. That means providing more funding for affordable housing, taking on corporate price-gouging, lowering prescription drug costs, raising wages, and protecting programs people rely on. We also need to stop pouring billions upon billions of dollars into endless wars while families here are struggling to survive. If we can always find money for destruction, we can find money to fully fund HUD, SNAP, and afford Medicare for All. Working people deserve a government that invests in their lives, not one that continues to ask them to do more with much less. Concerns about immigration drove much of the 2024 election, but the Trump administration's approach to enforcement since then has proven highly contentious. What approach do you favor for security at the border, and for immigrants who are living here already? No human being is illegal. Our immigration system is broken because it treats human beings like problems to punish instead of people deserving dignity, safety, and rights. Immigrant families are essential to Western Pennsylvania, strengthening our communities, filling critical jobs, and helping our region thrive. We need real pathways to citizenship, protections for Dreamers and TPS holders, fair asylum processes, and an end to family separation and abusive enforcement. I’ll keep fighting for an immigration system rooted in humanity, due process, and justice, one that sees our neighbors as human beings, not political targets. Artificial intelligence is said to be a game-changing technology, and the construction of data centers themselves can generate jobs. But there are concerns that the centers may drive up utility costs and disrupt communities, and that AI could cause massive social disruptions. What do you think federal policy toward AI should be? Federal policy on AI must put people first. We should be leaders in innovation, but never at the expense of human dignity, fairness, or justice. As AI grows, we need strong guardrails to prevent bias, discrimination, worker exploitation, and prepare for workforce disruption. AI should not increase utility prices nor destroy the environment. We need more transparency, accountability, and community input before we move forward with data centers. The economic gains of AI must benefit working families, not just enrich Big Tech billionaires. Technology should serve the public good, not just corporate profit. Democrats hope to retake the House this year, regaining control of at least one part of the federal government. If they do so, what should be their top priority — and how much faith do you have in the party's current leadership to pursue that agenda? If Democrats retake the House, the top priority must be using every tool we have to protect working people, defend our rights, and restore accountability. Congress cannot keep ceding its power while chaos, corruption, and cruelty go unchecked. We need real oversight, a serious agenda to lower costs and protect our communities, and the courage to stand up to abuses of power wherever they come from. This is not a moment for timid leadership or political convenience. It is a moment to be clear, bold, and accountable to the people we serve. A candidate whose previous runs include an earlier bid for the 12th Congressional District, Parker is known for a provocative style that sometimes features bringing his signature bullhorn to political events. He opened this year’s primary election season by challenging election petitions for both U.S. Rep. Chris Deluzio in the 17th District and his own opponent, U.S. Rep. Summer Lee. That challenge failed, as did an effort to remove Parker from the ballot, leaving Parker and Lee to face each other on May 19. Current occupation: Business owner, Vendspin Founder & CEO Related experience: Parker describes himself as an economic justice and Stop the Violence advocate, with over two decades of experience delivering fresh food and essentials to underserved communities. Supporters/endorsements: Parker identified only one endorser: “GOD.” Total raised: None reported.Total spent: None reported. What is the most important thing the federal government could do for your district, and how would you make it happen if you were elected to Congress? The most important thing the federal government can do for our district is invest in entrepreneurs, good jobs, infrastructure, and affordable homeownership. In Congress, I would push to secure our fair share of federal infrastructure and housing funds, work across the aisle on targeted appropriations, and partner with local officials, unions, business leaders and community groups to ensure shovel-ready projects. When I’m canvassing throughout District 12, the number one concern I hear is that there aren't enough recreational spaces for the youth. So, the first thing I want to work on when I get to Congress is bringing federal funding to build a state-of-the-art roller skating rink in Hazelwood Green. Polling consistently shows many voters are worried about the cost of living, especially when it comes to essentials such as housing and food. How do you think Congress should address these concerns? Families in our district feel squeezed from every direction. Congress needs to treat the cost of living as a kitchen-table emergency, not a talking point. I support expanding the Child Tax Credit and Earned Income Tax Credit, which have proven to reduce child poverty and put money directly back into working families’ pockets. We should increase federal support for affordable homeownership, including incentivizing building more units and preserving existing homes. I would protect and strengthen SNAP and school meal programs, and support antitrust enforcement to promote more competition in the food processing and grocery industry. We also need to raise the federal minimum wage and protect workers’ rights. Concerns about immigration drove much of the 2024 election, but the Trump administration's approach to enforcement since then has proven highly contentious. What approach do you favor for security at the border, and for immigrants who are living here already? We need an immigration system that is orderly, humane, and consistent with our values and laws. The status quo — lurching between cruelty and chaos — serves no one. I support modern, smart border security: more personnel and technology to disrupt drug and human trafficking, efficient processing, and clear, enforced rules. For immigrants already living here, especially those who have been in our communities for years, we need a path to legal status and citizenship for people who pass background checks, pay taxes, and are contributing to our economy. I’d support a bipartisan package combining border security, legal pathways, and humane treatment instead of using immigrants as political props. Artificial intelligence is said to be a game-changing technology, and the construction of data centers themselves can generate jobs. But there are concerns that the centers may drive up utility costs and disrupt communities, and that AI could cause massive social disruptions. What do you think federal policy toward AI should be? We need transparency about how AI systems are trained and used, protections against discrimination and surveillance, and accountability when AI systems cause harm — especially in areas like housing, hiring, credit, and the criminal legal system. Workers whose jobs are at risk from automation should have access to retraining, wage insurance, and strong unions. On data centers, I support federal incentives that require local hiring, fair wages, environmental safeguards, and coordination with utilities so new facilities don’t spike rates or strain the grid. We should encourage data centers to use clean energy, invest in grid resilience, and respect local land-use decisions. AI and data infrastructure should benefit communities, not extract from them. Democrats hope to retake the House this year, regaining control of at least one part of the federal government. If they do so, what should be their top priority — and how much faith do you have in the party's current leadership to pursue that agenda? If Democrats retake the House, the top priority should be a pro-people, pro-community, pro-democracy agenda people can feel in their daily lives. That means lowering costs for families, protecting democracy and fundamental rights, including voting rights and reproductive freedom, and investing in jobs and climate resilience. I believe the party’s leadership has shown it can govern and pass major legislation, but I also think we need to listen more closely to working people and younger voters who feel left out or disillusioned. I would be an independent voice within the caucus — willing to work with leadership when they’re advancing a bold, practical agenda, and willing to push them when they’re not. Have a tip for the WESA newsroom? Email news@wesa.fm.

title: Coming to Xbox Game Pass: Kiln, Aphelion, Final Fantasy V, and More - Xbox Wire
content: What do Chocobos, space, vampires, and sentient pottery have in common? If it’s anything more than “themes of games you can play soon” we’d really like to know what kind of weird list that is. Also, don’t forget to tune in to the ID@Xbox and IGN showcase to see spotlights on a some upcoming games (including some coming day one to Game Pass like There Are No Ghosts at the Grand, Aphelion, and Mistfall Hunter)! Go grab your keyboard and controller and let’s get to the games. Little Rocket Lab (Cloud, Console, and PC) – April 21Now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass Little Rocket Lab is a cozy, machine building RPG. You play as Morgan, an aspiring engineer, who heads home to St. Ambroise to help complete their family’s dream-building a rocket. Help the locals with new machines, be creative, and become the ultimate engineer! That rocket isn’t going to build itself. Sopa: Tale of the Stolen Potato (Cloud, Console, Handheld, and PC) – April 21Now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass Miho steps into the pantry to fetch a potato for his grandma and falls into a magical world full of wonders. Meet quirky characters, venture through the depths of South America, and learn the importance of the things we pass along in this unforgettable emotional narrative adventure game. Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 21Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one on Xbox Game Pass! From the creators of Vampire Survivors, Vampire Crawlers turns the thrill of Survivors into a hyper turn‑based, rogue-lite card‑driven blobber. Build busted decks, explore familiar dungeons, and unleash world‑ending combos. Either take your time and be tactical or play turns as fast as you humanly can: The outcome is always accurate! Kiln (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 23Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass!Kiln is a pottery power‑fantasy celebrating both creativity and destruction — the pleasure of making beautiful things, then smashing them to smithereens. Create your pot, grab your friends, and throw down in online arenas, where the ceramic creations you sculpt become the bodies you bring into battle. Aphelion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 28Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Aphelion is a third-person sci-fi adventure developed by Don’t Nod (Jusant, Lost Records: Bloom & Rage). After crash-landing on a frozen planet, astronaut Ariane must traverse harsh terrain and shifting realities to save her injured partner, Thomas. Players control both characters in a story rooted in real-world science, created in partnership with the European Space Agency. Trepang2 (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 29Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, and PC Game Pass Become the ultimate badass in Trepang2: A gory, action-packed FPS set in the near future. Unleash all hell on your enemies, dodge bullets and leave a trail of destruction in this hardcore and frenetic shooter. Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era (Game Preview) (PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is the official prequel hailing back to the origins of the genre-defining, critically acclaimed series of turn-based strategy games. Raise grand armies and wield devastating spells to overcome your foes in both solo and multiplayer. Sledding Game (Game Preview) (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Available day one with Xbox Game Pass! Sledding Game is a multiplayer snow-sports hangout with proximity voice chat. Race downhill, pull off ridiculous tricks, build ramps, or just relax with friends. Customize your animal avatar, jump into public or private lobbies, and enjoy chaotic ragdoll crashes — just watch out for the yeti. TerraTech Legion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass Build the ultimate battle vehicle and rage against the machines in a chaotic Bullet Heaven Builder. Master a deep, block-based vehicle-building system to survive an army of robotic foes driven by a crazed AI, ramming them at speed and shooting infinite projectiles. Final Fantasy V (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – May 5Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, PC Game Pass When the crystals that balance the world are threatened, the king hastens to the rescue…only to go missing. A young man and his Chocobo find themselves drawn toward friends that will change their destiny in this classic RPG. The following games are leaving the Game Pass library soon. Jump back in to tie up any loose ends or save up to 20% off your purchase to keep the fun going! Go pick out what game you’re going to play next, and we’ll meet you back here to tell you what else is “Coming Soon”! Cheers! Note: Games with a ‘Handheld’ designation represent those that are optimized for handheld play.

title: Vote for Section V's Boys Athlete of the Week for April 13-19 presented by Faber Builders - Democrat and Chronicle
content: It's time to take a look at the standout performers from Section V. Your vote will determine who will be the Faber Builders Boys Sports Athlete of the Week for April 13-19. The players' credentials are listed above the poll — which will be live until 8 p.m. Wednesday. The winner will be announced on our Instagram page Thursday morning. The nominees are chosen by our sports reporters. If you would like to call attention to an athlete's performance to be considered for the nomination, please email sports@democratandchronicle.com by 7 p.m. each Sunday. All athletes who play on Section V boys teams are eligible to be nominated. The poll can be found at the bottom of the article. Now, let's meet the candidates. Spencerport junior shot the best score during a golf match against Brockport, an impressive two-under par 34 on the final nine holes at Mill Creek Golf Course. Fairport senior became the first track and field athlete in Section V this season to run a 800-meter race in less than two minutes, when he crossed the finish line at the Webster Invitational in 1 minute, 59.67 seconds. A senior at Pittsford Sutherland, who shot a score of 34, an impressive one-under par, over nine holes at Caledonia Country Club. Honeoye Falls-Lima baseball player pitched a complete game (seven innings), a shutout with 12 strikeouts and two hits and had six hits in nine at-bats during the week. Victor boys lacrosse player tallied six goals with one assist in the Blue Devils’ win against Canandaigua, in a rematch of last season’s Class B section final. McQuaid boys lacrosse goalie racked up 54 saves across three games last week, two of which were wins for the Knights. That includes a career-high 25 saves in Tuesday’s overtime loss to Irondequoit. Batavia-Notre Dame boys lacrosse player recorded five goals and one assist while finishing 12-for-12 at the faceoff X in his team’s win against Gananda. Livonia-Avon-Geneseo boys lacrosse eighth-grader had eight goals and one assist in his team’s win against Haverling. Irondequoit boys lacrosse player had the OT winner in the Eagles’ victory against McQuaid. Velepec finished the game with two goals.

title: Joan V. Roehrs Obituary April 20, 2026 - Krill Funeral Service
content:
Joan V. Roehrs, age 80,of Bryan, passed away Monday, April 20, 2026 at her home. Joan worked as a crossing guard for the Bryan Police Department for many years. She enjoyed fishing, camping, spending time with her grandchildren and attending their sporting events. Joan was born on September 17, 1945, in Bryan, the daughter of Harvey and Katie (Figley) Hanna. She married Robert A. Roehrs on October 2, 1964, in Bryan, and he preceded her in death on May 6, 2021. Joan is survived by her children, Robert Roehrs, Jr., of Montpelier, Austina Walker and Mary Favourite, both of Bryan, George (Glenda) Roehrs, of Edgerton and Lewis Roehrs, of Bryan; seven grandchildren, Heather (Dustin) Hartsock, Nick (Vanessa) Walker, Alyssa Favourite, Matthew (Morgan) Favourite, Tenika Roehrs, Trevor Roehrs and Katelynn Lamnick; six great grandchildren; brother, George Hanna, of Bryan; brother-in-law, Byron “Butch” Beltz, of Fort Wayne and many nieces and nephews Along with her husband she was preceded in death by her parents; sisters, Alice Hanna, Sarah Beltz and Janet (Jim) Roehrs and sister-in-law, Pat Hanna. In accordance with Joan’s wishes, there will be no visitation or services held. Arrangements have been entrusted to Krill Funeral Service, 860 West Mulberry Street, Bryan. Memorial donations are requested to Shop with a Cop. A beautifully bound keepsake book filled with memories and condolences from family and friends. Contribute your memory to the online guestbook to be featured in this unique keepsake book. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA and the  Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply. title: V: ‘I Gave My All to Create This Album’ - Rolling Stone
content:
V
 spent much of his childhood with his grandmother in Geochang, a small town in southern South Korea. He came away with a taste for older music, from Chet Baker to Frank Sinatra to Elvis Presley, and a broody, smoldering vibe that stands out among his bandmates. He has a voice to match, a smoky baritone that he lets loose on his vibe-y, bedroom-eyed debut EP, Layover. He’s always been an essential element in BTS’ sound, and on Arirang, his songwriting contributions included essential tracks like “Into the Sun.” He seems quietly confident about it all, with the exception of his inability to hit the kind of high notes that come so naturally to the likes of Jimin. “I am on the lower end,” he says, in a February interview conducted in Hybe’s Seoul, South Korea headquarters. “But I’ve learned a lot. I’ve gotten better since learning from our past albums, but it will always be a challenge for me.” How did you feel in 2022, when it became clear a break was coming? This is all you’ve known for so long.Well, while we’re in the military, we can’t perform even if we want to. So I did really yearn to perform. I wanted to release an album, I wanted to sing and dance. I definitely felt that … desire? Let’s call it desire. But since we were needed by our country, we had to do the best with our situation at the time. So I tried very hard to forget about V of BTS for a while, so that I could concentrate on living my life as Kim Taehyung. You said that after discharge, you rebuilt and reset your mind and body. What does that mean to you?That idea was so important to me. I knew that my time in the military was going to be quite long, so I thought very hard about how to spend it well and not waste it away. First off, I considered my values and my health. I tried to organize and give a lot of thought to my inner world, looking back on myself and contemplating the kind of person I ultimately want to become. So for that year and a half, I worked out a whole lot. Read a lot, listened to a lot of music. And I think that gave me the opportunity to rebuild my body and mind. What’s the most important thing you read?I asked my friend: “Hey, I want to read! Do you have any books to recommend?” And they got so excited and recommended Han Kang’s and Keigo Higashino’s books. So that’s literally all I read. There was also a book called Eleven Steps. It was kind of a philosophy book. But with any of the different books, I found myself putting myself into the text. Like imagining myself as a character in a novel, for example. I was so deep in my imagination at the time. Was it helpful? I’m not sure. ARMY says you seem more confident. Do you feel that way?It’s just that … Actually, I found that after working out, I looked and felt different from when I didn’t work out, both on the outside and inside. So I guess I have a more confident figure. You are so careful with your solo music. You have a lot of songs that you make, and then you don’t put out or you delete. What did it mean to get Layover finished and out into the world?Layover came to me at a time when I felt the need to look back on my journey so far as V of BTS. The word “layover” can mean a stop between destinations, but to me, it’s expressing that my life is not a straight path. I want to say: I can make this kind of music, but I also like many other styles. I love jazz, classical music, and even more alternative music, so I wanted to share that with ARMY and our listeners. I genuinely love music with all my heart, and Layover is that representation of me. The new BTS album is very different from your solo work. Going into it, what were your personal goals for the album?First and most importantly, there’s not a single song on this album that is not my style. Every single one is my style and the kind of music I’ve wanted to make. I think this was another color I wanted to pursue, in terms of genre. So I gave it my all to create this album, and it has a lot of love in it. This Arirang album, I mean. Do you have a favorite moment, whether your own or from another member, on this album?Since we worked on so many songs, there were plenty of songs I loved that didn’t get picked for the album. And of course I have my favorites among the songs that got included. But more than anything … I can’t wait to be onstage again. Because my favorite songs always change when we get to perform. In a group of seven members, there’s only so much sonic real estate. Does ego ever get involved — “I would like to be more on this song,” or “This song where I’m featured more didn’t make it”?Well, I think I was more worried about being able to do a good job. Because no matter how much I listen to what I like, it’s a totally different game to perform it myself. So even if I love something, I have to try very, very hard to interpret and reimagine it in a way that makes sense for me to do. But if I ever have fewer parts or I’m taking a less central part in a song, I don’t feel bad about that. Because another member will take that place. I just feel … relieved, in a way. The only thing is, if the seven of us don’t mesh, we just pass on that song. I think the group is good at making everyone feel pretty much equal. Right. And since all seven of us have had a solo career, everyone’s individual ego is stronger now. I thought that might change things when we got together again, but we actually amplified each other and ended up making a better album. So I get the sense that our work is more polished than before. Would it be accurate to say you have zero desire to be a solo pop star?If I have zero desire, shouldn’t I stop being an artist? I learned so much from [the other members] while working on this album and got a lot out of observing their paths. I really learned a lot. So when the time comes to make my next solo album, I plan to absorb every member’s biggest strengths. I love the jazzier sound on something like the solo track “Winter Ahead,” which is a really beautiful song. But it sounds like you may be open to making a pop album.Yeah. Even that is a style of music I love — one that I’ve worked to pursue. So, of course. I don’t know when that would be, but someday, it’s a genre that I’d have to and would love to try. That’s what I think. ARMY loves your acting, but they would like you to play a part where the character doesn’t die. Will you do more of it, and will you seek out a part where the character gets to live?I think my mindset is the same in my acting as in my music. When I’m acting, part of the point is to try a bunch of different parts that I can’t experience in reality, right? So in the same way, I don’t want to limit myself to one genre of music. I want to experience every single genre I really love. And someday, if I get the chance, I hope I can act in a lot of different parts that I’ve wanted to try out. If there’s one jazz artist you want ARMY to get into, who would it be?Oh, there’s too many! Etta James! Styling by YEJIN KIM. Hair by HANSOM, HWAYEON, and HYUNWOO LEE. Makeup by DAREUM KIM and SHINAE.Set design by YEABYUL JEON. Produced by NUHANA. Executive Producer SOOH HWANG. Producers SEBIN PARK and KALY NGO. Line producer CHERRY LEE. Digital Technician HUIJIN KIM. Photographic Assistance: SOOJUNG OH, MINHYUK LEE, MINJUN KIM, JIHYUN OH, JUWAN KANG and JUNHYUNG YANG. Set Design Team: SOHYUN WON, YUNSEON CHOI, JUNHYUK SIM. RS Video DoP MIKE BEECH. Camera Operators: BYEONG HWI MIN, CHURL GWON, HYUNSUH PAIK. DIT JIWOON LEE. Sound operator MIN JAE LEE. Production assistant SEOHYUN YOONClothing CreditsV: Jacket by BONBOM. title: Apollo v Artemis: How the Earth changed in 58 years - BBC
content: After the Apollo 8 crew captured the iconic Earthrise photo in 1968, Artemis astronauts have recreated the image, revealing changes to our fragile blue planet. When the commander of Apollo 8, Frank Borman, first saw the far side of the Moon from his spacecraft window in 1968 he was struck by its desolate appearance. "The lunar surface was terribly distressed with meteorite craters and volcanic residue," he told me during a BBC interview in 2018. "It was either grey or black or white – there was absolutely no colour on the lunar surface, and it was messed up beyond belief." But as the spacecraft completed its fourth lunar orbit, suddenly, a very different view appeared. "We looked up and there was the Earth in the background coming over the lunar surface, and Bill Anders took the picture that became probably one of the more significant pictures that humans have ever taken," Borman said. "The Earth was the only thing in the entire Universe that had any colour, it was a remarkable sight – we're very, very fortunate to live on this planet." This Earthrise picture, as it was soon named, became one of the most reproduced images of all time. Showing our planet in the context of the lunar wasteland and vast oblivion of space, it galvanised the environmental movement, leading to the creation of Earth Day in 1970. (Read more about the 1968 photo that changed the world). Fifty-eight years later, Nasa astronauts have taken another striking photo of the Earth dipping below a barren lunar landscape: "Earthset". During their fly-by of the Moon earlier this month, the Artemis II crew captured the new image of our fragile blue planet in the vast expanse of space. (We don't know who took the picture this time because the four astronauts have chosen not to credit photos to individuals but to the whole crew). In geological terms, just over half a century is barely the shadow of a blink of an eye. But climate change has altered the Earth's surface significantly over the past six decades. Experts tell the BBC about the visible differences between the Earthrise and Earthset photos and what these tell us about our planet then and now. Despite its impact and legacy, what's so surprising about the Earthrise picture is that no one at Nasa had seen it coming. "They caught it by accident, right?" says US astronaut Sian Proctor, the pilot for the first wholly "civilian" mission to space (named Inspiration). "Apollo 8 changed the way we saw our planet and that's the kind of thing I feel like we need right now, more inspiration." When I asked a question at the press conference following the Artemis launch about plans for a new Earthrise picture, it was clear that, this time, Nasa was not going to be caught out. "We're going to do everything we can to try and make that happen," said Lori Glaze, who leads the agency's Exploration Systems Development Mission Directorate. The Earthset photo was captured through the Orion spacecraft window 18:41 Eastern Daylight Time (23:41BST) on 6 April, during a seven-hour flyby of the Moon. "The Earth's sunlit side shows white clouds and blue water over the Oceania region, while the dark areas are experiencing nighttime. The image also shows incredible detail of the Moon's surface and its overlapping craters and basins," Nasa notes in its description of the image. Unlike in 1968, numerous satellites take thousands of images of our planet each day in 2026. They measure and monitor our oceans, land and ice in all shades of the electromagnetic spectrum – from microwave to ultraviolet – not just what we can see with our eyes. There is a continuous video feed from the International Space Station and robotic spacecraft have even captured the Earth from the vantage point of the Moon and beyond, but the fact that Earthset was taken by humans sets it apart. Craig Donlon, who oversees the plans for the next generation of satellites at the European Space Agency (Esa), says humans give us a different perspective. "The images that you get from a human are framed, focused, and there are choices that are made subconsciously and consciously by the astronaut when they press the shutter, they've got something in the back of their mind," Donlon says. "That forces some kind of emotion, which says, 'Well, wow, okay, little old Earth there, but that's where we live, that's it, that's everything.'" But it's not just the human connection that makes the Earthrise and Earthset images significant. Despite being snapshots taken some 58 years apart, they can also help reveal how the Earth has changed in that time. "Since Earthrise, atmospheric carbon dioxide levels have risen by about a third and global temperatures have warmed rapidly, by at least 1C," says Richard Allan, a professor of climate science at the National Centre for Earth Observation at the University of Reading in the UK. "The planet has transformed as human activities alter the texture of our land as seen from space, expanding cities, clearing dark forests for replacement by bright agricultural lands and contributing to the desiccation of the Aral Sea which has shrunk to less than 10% of its 1960s size." Some of that change can even be seen in the images themselves, even though the Earth is shrouded in cloud. "Although they're of different parts of the Earth, the one thing that does show up in both images, although you're looking at different parts of it, is Antarctica and the Southern Ocean," says Benjamin Wallis, a glaciologist at the University of Leeds in the UK. "The Antarctic Peninsula is one of the most rapidly warming parts of the Earth and 28,000km of ice shelf have collapsed in between the original image and the latest image." Studies suggest these changes in the ice around Antarctica are unprecedented in the last 10,000 years. Other areas of the Earth where water exists in a solid form – known as the cryosphere – have been similarly affected. "We really have seen some dramatic changes," says Petra Heil, director of science at the British Antarctic Survey. "We have now seen in both hemispheres a dramatic decrease in the seasonal sea ice cover, and in North America, Eurasia and Asia, we have seen much later seasonal snow cover, and we also see it melting earlier." "I think we are pretty confident based on the observations but also the numerical models to assign probably 90-95% of the change to human activities," Heil says. But while all this might make for grim reading, it's worth remembering that in 1968 – despite appearances from deep space – we had already caused damage to the planet. • Mercury to Artemis: The evolution of mission control "[Earthrise] left many people enthralled with the idea of how beautiful the Earth was and how much damage was being done," says Kathleen Rogers, president of the Earth Day network. "I remember at the time you couldn't see across the street in LA at rush hour because of smog and our rivers were on fire." "From so far away, the Earth looks so perfect and so beautiful and then as you drew closer, you could see the ravages of 150 years of, let's call it, progress," says Rogers. "But it did inspire a generation of people to really step it up and become part of a movement." Borman died in 2023, but the legacy of his Apollo 8 mission still endures and his words are just as relevant today for a new generation of lunar astronauts: "I don't think any of us have paid any attention to the fact that we would be going all the way to the Moon and be more interested in looking at the Earth". For essential climate news and hopeful developments to your inbox, sign up to the Future Earth newsletter, while The Essential List delivers a handpicked selection of features and insights twice a week. For more science, technology, environment and health stories from the BBC, follow us on Facebook and Instagram. With storms occurring between 140 to 160 nights a year, it's no wonder the area is a world record holder. Tech Now meets a startup trying to fix one of the fashion industry's biggest blind spots, inconsistent sizing. A gold‑medalist has developed a vibrating starting block to give deaf athletes clearer, fairer race starts. In Tokyo, BBC TechXplore tests live translation and AI-powered displays that makes the city more navigable. At Tokyo's Deaflympics, deaf Judo fans aren't just watching the matches, they're feeling them, thanks to Hapbeat. Under the surface of the ocean, 100,000 towering mountains dot the Earth. Their violent currents are key to life. Musician KT Tunstall meets her avatar as Tech Now explores music’s virtual future. Rollercoasters send our hearts racing and stomachs dropping. Hannah Fry dives into the science of how they work. Discover how quirky clips paved the way for viral humour, proving randomness never goes out of style. The Japanese photographer who buried hundreds of photos and the journey to revive his legacy. From a shipwrecking yard in Bangladesh to a river of iron dioxide in Canada, a deep dive in Ed Burtynsky's work. Black holes are one of the mysteries of the universe where all the laws of nature as we know them stop working. The war in the Middle East has brought renewed attention to Britain's vulnerability to energy price shocks. The 4,000-year-old drug, most commonly used to treat pain, prevents certain tumours from forming and spreading across the body – findings that are already changing health policies. On a misty morning in December, two photographers captured the images of 304 individual humpbacks – the highest number of large whales ever identified in a single day.
