title: V.com weekend vote: At what age did you start playing the violin? - Violinist.com
content: We have thousands of human-written stories, discussions, interviews and reviews from today through the past 20+ years. Find them here: April 18, 2026, 7:52 PM · Last week an article about starting the violin as a senior citizen inspired a robust conversation about people's experiences, starting the violin at an advanced age. It would seem that quite a lot of people have started playing their instrument late in life, so I wanted to re-do a vote we've done before: at what age did you start playing the violin? I have added a "senior citizen" category: people who started over the age of 65. It's a great chance to talk about when all of us started, and I predict that the answers will be all over the map! Since the advent of the Suzuki method, it's not unusual to start at a very young age, even two or three. But you can enjoy this instrument starting at any age, and with the key ingredients of motivation and consistent practice, you can get quite good at it. Personally, I started violin a few days shy of my ninth birthday, in a public school program at Eastridge Elementary School in Aurora, Colorado. Thank you Mrs. Linda Walker, for inspiring young me to beg my parents to join your program! How old were you when you started playing the violin (or viola), and what was that initial spark that got you going? Please participate in the vote, and then tell us all about it in the comments! April 19, 2026 at 12:10 AM · I started violin when I was 7, but I honestly don't remember why. I think someone suggested it, but I honestly don't recall. I had done piano for a few years by that point. I ended up loving both piano and violin and took lessons on both throughout grade school. I also added viola when I was 12. April 19, 2026 at 01:30 AM · I was 7yrs old when I started. My parents took me to a local concert. I remember being mesmerized by the sound of the violin, and how much I enjoyed watching the emotions displayed by the violinists while playing. I also remember enjoying watching the conductor conduct.. lol. After the concert, I asked my parents if I can learn to play the violin, a few weeks later, my Mom introduced me to my first Suzuki violin teacher, and the rest is history. Sometimes I wonder if that was the intent of my parents all along.. lol. Sidenote: My kids started to learn the violin when they turned 3 1/2 yrs old with a Suzuki trained teacher. They wanted to learn because they hear me play all their lives, and was attracted to the sound of the instrument and music in general. April 19, 2026 at 01:35 AM · I started at 5, stopped after high school and started picking it up about a  years and half ago. It's been very slow picking it back up with no time to practice but I hope to practice more and with a purpose now that I have my dream violin and a pretty good bow. April 19, 2026 at 07:01 AM · Aged 11 I started at a boarding school and was given a shotgun choice between the violin and the recorder (which of course is a girl's instrument so no choice really). At a day school (or at a later age) I wouldn't have had that compulsion and today I'd be a completely different person. April 19, 2026 at 10:36 AM · I started playing the fiddle at 60... but moved back to playing classical music at about aged 64.It was a much bigger challenged than I expected but 8 years later I am really enjoying playing second violin in a local orchestra, and in a small strings chamber group. I had played the flute from aged 9, then the oboe, piano, wooden traditional flute and concertina, all instruments with reasonable set or fixed intonation. Nothing prepared me for the difficulty of learning to play the violin in tune, but it has been one of the most rewarding experiences of my life. April 19, 2026 at 12:17 PM · I requested during the school year of my 9th birthday, started lessons on my 10th. Most other people in my class wanted to play the guitar, so me wanting to be different, chose the violin. At the time, I didn't realise how undifferent it was lmao. April 19, 2026 at 01:19 PM · I started at 58 motivated by Western Swing, a declining uke group, Prok 2, Bartok's string quartets (Guarneri quartet), an interest in classical music since I was 5 and the realisation that violin sections are the biggest orchestral sections, increasing my chances of finding a place. April 19, 2026 at 01:55 PM · Both my parents were violinists, and a baby class was offered at the University of Texas, Austin. I vividly remember playing on the stage at the concert when I was 2. April 19, 2026 at 05:23 PM · Reply{#9} Elisabeth Matesky, "Apostle" of Jascha Heifetz and Nathan Milstein, Yet I began playing the Violin on my *Third Birthday with Poppa Ralph Matesky, Juilliard Top of Class Grad & honoured Violin String Teacher for an entire District of major City Schools Music Programmes & whom Laurie Niles knows via her 1st acquaintances with the Violin & I believe other String Cousins!! I was inspired by both my parents & professional musicians highly regarded throughout the major city they lived in when hearing/listening at 2 to Momma on the Piano (Arnold Schoenberg's UCLA Advanced Classes Pianist) and Poppa, Superb Violinist/Pupil of both Piastro and Ysaye 'Apostle' Eduoard Dethier at the Juilliard School of Music, with both reading through Violin & Piano Sonatas at home hearing beautiful sounds aged 2. I am told and was forever informed I loved the Piano and Violin asking for a "Biolin" (unable at aged 2 to pronounce Violin properly) and on my Third Birthday gifted a baby violin in a black case with a doted top of the violin case with a beautiful green colour inside where my new baby violin sat with a bow. I can still See the Scene in my mind & even now knowing it was so special a Birthday to me, just a child yet loving music of my parents who always read Brahms, Mozart and LvB plus other Composers unknown to me at that early time in my little life, but loving the sounds and watching beloved parents making music together and it always seemed with Love! I never recall an argument between my parents over Music: Never. Their Example brought me much Joy as I started growing up and wanting to play in the elementary school orchestra which I had to audition or "Try Out" for and then invited to become a Member of the Second Violins Last Stand and truly thrilled to be a part of an orchestra! I learnt many Violin II Orchestral Parts which was So Good for a child with Big Ears and Love for Music which I truly do believe to This Writing Is Good for All People and also now discovering by Autism Experts Health Aide for Children diagnosed with a form of severe "non verbal" unable autism who cannot communicate with words But 'if learning an Instrument Can Learn to "Feel and Love Sounds" on a musical instrument and can learn to Play a Violin if taught with great Love and Patience plus some sort of Common Sense backgrounds, Musically-Medically. Only Yesterday, I was reading an Article about this Subject and now inspired having met last month a Mom with a severely non verbal child of 9 with Autism who needs major Help to Self-Help her beloved Child and this site for Strings is a boost Today with many replying and I am sure More to come! Re: Learning to play a violin or String Cousin (Viola, Violoncello or Double Bass or Classical Guitar) over 65, I think a very healthy approach to getting older or succinctly put: "More Mature!" for those intrigued or just plain curious about How to Play the Violin or other String Cousins here mentioned!! It is Not an Activity to be Judged by any Teacher. This is a paramount Ingredient to Start Day One with and to Not try to "Teach" Every Thing in a First Day Lesson/ First Acquaintance with a person 65 or Over even 80 or 90!!!!! The Idea is to Encourage and with tons of Knowledge but Not to foist all knowledge on a beginner who has lived on Planet Earth for some time and often longer than a String Teacher interested or curious to Try to teach an older person! I do believe Music is for Every Person on Earth and our glorious Ages Old Composers for Every Generation and depending on their place of Birth re Specific Culture if not exposed to Violin Playing in the "Traditional" Way most Western Countries know it to be but for adaptations of such if from Countries with Ages Old Instruments resembling Western Violins and the playing of all Violins made in many Centuries but when starting out best to Rent a Violin even if aged 65 or Older to Not incur major Costs and unknown unless a very experienced Violin or other String Cousin Teacher is there and to Not make 'A Deal" to get a certain Percentage Back from a Said String Dealer or Shop. This is Not an Idea, Period to Use a New Pupil and never having played the Violin or other String Instruments when first starting out with most not less unknowing of Instrument Costs and varying on which City or Town one lives in??? In my 6.5 decades teaching A-Z experience & starting at aged 8 with Poppa RM giving me 5 elementary school pupils in our neighbourhood to teach once a week, after 5 years learning Violin from Poppa R, a Splendid Teacher of All US Youth and Founder of "The US Youth Orchestra Movement", then known for this Nationally and later Internationally, (tbc) he taught me to Not try to teach "too much too soon" and allowed me to listen in to his own teaching Privately at Home which was a major eye opener to my Idea of How To Teach including many variations on the Theme of Violin and Music Mentoring ... I encourage All beginning Violin & String Cousin Pupils to find an experienced teacher via your fine Local Orchestra or if in a Major City ringing the Major Orchestra for Help to find a known of experienced teacher who has ALSO taught those with disabilities, aka, Autism, to teach beginning pupils including Adults 65 and Up!! This will allow some very fine Professional Violinists/ String Players in the Orchestra to assess the Needs more appropriately and Not RX a Major Orchestra Concertmaster who is overloaded with Orchestral Concertmaster Responsibilities and many of whom I know well yet haven't the extra Time to Help-Love unless knowing of an Autism Family with a child suffering from this still very darkened illness yet being more & more studied in depth currently. A Word: One Other Idea; If a known & revered Retired or On Leave Major Orchestra Concertmaster is taking a Sabbatical then it might be an intriguing Idea to contact said person to ask some Help and/or Referrals to a Colleague!! Reading all of the other 9 Replies I am most intrigued reading all of them thus far and will Return possibly manana to write a bit more and of my own learned via International Teaching Violin/et al {Ch Mus/Violoncello/Viola plus Piano Collaborators Experienced Observations and I wish to Thank Laurie Niles for her most thoughtful Article having Voted in Box 'Aged 5 & Under' due starting Violin at 3 Years of age on an October Birthday with Poppa Ralph Matesky then on to Jascha Heifetz, and privately with Only Peer of Heifetz, also Friend, Nathan Milstein 3.5 Yrs in London & NM Assist-Help in Zurich 1970s Summers! ~ ~ ~ Sending All Newer Violinists The Best ~ ~ ~ ....... Elisabeth Matesky, Advanced yet Part I! ....... Fwd ~ dgl SAVE Book File Beginning Violin3-65 Plus EM Reply {#9} April 19, 2026 at 06:29 PM · Started violin at 16 and viola at 18, self-taught for a long time. Had my first lessons at 33 and started regular lessons at 38. I already played piano and had played low brass in school bands. I discovered classical radio when I was in middle school, and then wanted to learn the viola after hearing the Walton viola concerto at the first orchestral concert I attended. I started on violin instead of viola mostly because I could get a violin for free, one that had belonged to a long-deceased great-uncle. I resorted to self-teaching after being turned away by all of the teachers my parents asked, all saying I was too old to learn a string instrument. But I played in community orchestras from quite early on, so I was able to get some pointers that way. April 19, 2026 at 06:31 PM · Steve, a girl’s instrument? What? I have noticed that I’ve seen only women play the harp. Does that mean the harp is only for us angels? I started the violin at about age 20. I had always admired Bob Wills,  for his Western swing style. One day, while I was supposed to be registering for a new college semester, I woke up sick as hell. My bestie went and signed me up in my choices. But…..he came home with a case, and inside was a student violin and a bow. He had signed me up for Violin 101. The second time I started was at age 73. One of my bandmates kept saying, “there’s a good fiddle break in this song”, and staring at me. So, another student fiddle and bow came into my life. Sometimes I want to quit, but I never regret it. Here’s one thing I don’t get. Kids can start on violin at age 2, and succeed. So, how come we can’t get kids under age 6 to learn anything on the ukulele, other than whacking a sibling over the head with it? April 19, 2026 at 07:34 PM · I had been violin curious my entire life, but my mom signed me up for piano at 6, starting in a music 1-12 school in Romania. I loved it but it did not last. My dad was sent abroad for a few years and the family followed. I spent the rest of my childhood trying to get back to my happy place, the music school, but .. we kept traveling. In high school, I finally gave up the idea, signed up for a choral a capella group to meet my music needs and put my brain into math and physics. I have a long career in the engineering field, and now live in the US. When the kids were old enough to play, I tried along the clarinet with one (extremely hard!) and the cello. And at that point I realized I could try to do what I never thought I could before, learning to play the violin. Covid came and went, I had little success with the online learning, so the year after things opened up, I walked into a music and arts store and found the greatest teacher. Within 7 months, I very tentatively joined a community orchestra. 3 years in, I am taking music classes at the community college, play in a high level orchestra ( we play things like Brahms, Beethoven or Mozart Symphonies, have Pops concerts several times a year and play chamber music in several groups. In a few weeks, I will play the Vivaldi Spring in the college student recital, to be followed by a jury evaluation. I am pinching myself every time I pick up the violin, and am so grateful for the community of musicians who have made this possible for me. April 19, 2026 at 07:37 PM · Age wise, I’m 58, started at 55. Planning to be that 90 year old relative who pulls out the violin at every party! April 20, 2026 at 07:10 AM · I was extremely fortunate to be living in Ottawa, Kansas, when Alice Joy Lewis was discovering the Suzuki movement. Her husband and my father were faculty colleagues at the local small college, and my oldest sister was taking traditional violin lessons from Alice Joy, so it was natural for my parents to sign me up for Alice Joy’s very first Suzuki class when it was announced. I was five, and thrilled at the thought of following in my sister’s footsteps. April 20, 2026 at 08:20 AM · @Andrew Hsieh ~ As from "Apostle" of Jascha Heifetz & Nathan Milstein, Violinist, Elisabeth Matesky {Reply #15} Re All Teachers asked by your parents turning away with the words "You're too old to learn a string instrument." In all my Life such Vicious Words being uttered to your loving parents about their requests for so-called 'teachers' to teach You, their Son, loving Music and the Violin, is beyond Bizarre and vicious which reminds me of those 'snob' teachers with "Gestalt" I've when all the time living in London, but Not In London or UK, but on the European Continent, who would use IMO very dire deliberately chosen words to refuse teaching those they felt "lower" in sophistication than their perception of themselves with None ever achieving anything near the Level's of my Violin Mentors, known Globally including my first teacher - my father Ralph Matesky, a revered Violinist from Juilliard Top of Class Graduate and noted quite early on in his musical career Supervisor of All Music in a huge District in Southern California in all elementary, junior high and senior high public schools, creating wondrous opportunities for those never having played a string or other orchestral instrument to learn How to Play a String Instrument or Brass or Woodwind or what is known as Percussion Instrumnents in Orchestral settings, so all young people wishing to learn and in so doing Join their School Orchestra, and growing immensely from Teaching with Love amid truthful knowledge of all Orchestral Instruments and Major Composer Scores plus Poppa R's many arrangements & compositions for all K through 12 grades and carrying forward to College/University/ many music conservatories beyond well thought out and inspired by Poppa R's Love for Young People and especially children, who taught at least 2 or 3 Generations of Kids from the Poorest Sides of a major city who turned out mostly well and adapting to Great Classical Music having been mentored with the attitude that All Great Music was for All People no matter background or if Poor or in the Middle or whatever which did NOT Matter according to Poppa RM who taught children and teens The Best and about The GOAT Composers of Centuries with Love and Patience for those beginners with sincere hearts to Learn How to Play a String Instrument and so successful as a School Text he later was published by major Music Publishing Houses in NYC, for his after 1st Book, 'How to Teach non String Players How to Teach Strings'!!! My Point: Please if at all possible totally Dismiss All Nays of those who were stuffed shirts who did Not Know How to teach You Where You Were at the given Time/s when your loving parents asked for Help. I will quote a phrase from one of my having become fabled Father/Music Educator in an Article he wrote in a major music magazine: "You can Not Teach That Which You Can NOT DO." Remember this @Andrew Hsieh /Never Forget the wise and experienced Words of "The Father of the US Youth Orchestra Movement", my Poppa Ralph Matesky, and Toss all those non educated 'snob' teachers aside for the rest of your life to carry on & with enjoyment and pride for your own self-taught skills unknown at the time yet I am quite sure having done remarkably well via Your Own Instincts amid true Love for Music and the Violin! A Word: Delighted for Romanian born @Ioana Sundius! Bravo for re-finding Your Happy Place via The Violin!! EM I must leave off and was forced to defer my EM Return Reply {#15} to now & having awakened in the middle of the night of 20th April, 2026 Finale!!! ~ ~ ~ Sending Both Compliments for Courage & Self Reliance ~ ~ ~ ... Elisabeth Matesky, Heifetz/Milstein Pupil Concert Violin Artist ... Fwd ~ dgl SAVE V.com/EM {Reply#15} Andrew Hsieh/Self-Helped Success *also Ioana Sundius April 20, 2026 at 08:48 AM · Lessons are crucial. In my first four years of playing I had a dozen "violin lessons" from a friend who was a professional violist. I just assumed I couldn't afford more, being a retired civil servant and having had free lessons as a child. Then I spent two years teaching myself bad habits. Then I had the sense to assemble a year's worth of bank statements and calculate my outgoings for that year. That was when I realised I could afford at least monthly lessons. My oboe teacher when I left school had told me adults only really needed monthly lessons. But most teachers seem to insist on weekly, so I negotiated fortnightly with an agency, and have now had an excellent teacher for just over 2 years. We are basically covering everything in Fischer's Basics, but she's doing it independently of him because she's Italian and hasn't heard of him. April 20, 2026 at 10:44 AM · I picked up the violin at age 22, and thought I had left it very late. April 20, 2026 at 11:56 AM · Full agreement with Elisabeth's firm condemnation of Andrew Hsieh's would-be teachers. I started at nine and experienced wonderful teaching for the first two years at a studio run by a brilliant family. I never subsequently found inspiring teaching. April 20, 2026 at 04:56 PM · Nickie - there had been an after-classes recorder group at my primary school, exclusively patronised by girls. We boys preferred football. April 20, 2026 at 07:12 PM · I remember crashing my primary school's xylophone group. The teacher wasn't happy. I wasn't good at the recorder either. Got a piano scholarship when I was 12. I was 13 when I took up the oboe. I took to football like a brick takes to water. April 20, 2026 at 09:12 PM · Age 7 after spending a year on the flute and then the year prior spending a year on the piano. Going through the other instruments was good in terms of learning to read music. Have to give props to my dad who suggested it after the first two didn’t work out! April 21, 2026 at 05:44 PM · My grandfather played Irish fiddle and attempted to teach my brothers and me when we were kids, but we only saw him about one week a year so none of that took root. Also, I was more interested in guitar (mostly classical), which I played into my 30s. I was pretty good. I worked in an acoustic music shop in my late 30s and although we didn't sell violins, we took two old fiddles in trade one day, and I decided that I'd finally pursue the Irish music thing so I bought the cheaper one of the fiddles and took it home. After about a year I decided that I really didn't enjoy fiddling, but I had always loved chamber music so I started trying to teach myself classical violin. It took about five years to find a teacher who would work with an adult (I was 43 by then), and I'd developed so many poor playing habits that I made very little progress with her and she encouraged me to find a new hobby. A few years later I found another teacher and she started me at close to zero and we went through a ton of beginner repertoire for a year before she retired. Nine years ago I was taken on by a very good teacher who helped me build proper technique. It was not always a pleasant journey, but I decided to just trust my teacher and do exactly what she told me to do, and I've made the transition from being stuck as a pretty bad beginner to being a decent intermediate player. There's a lot of repertoire I will never be able to touch, but at my current level I have loads of stuff I can play reasonably well: Mozart, Haydn, Bach, Schubert, some Beethoven, Dvorak, really a ton of music. Next fall I'll be 65 and will retire. I plan to spend a great deal of time playing chamber music. My skills are still improving, so although there were some very discouraging years along the way, I have no regrets about taking up the violin as an adult. I'm also working on my piano playing. Lots to do. April 21, 2026 at 08:23 PM · Feedback is the really crucial thing. One of the main reasons I was able to reach a pretty decent level without lessons (admittedly with some bad habits) was that I got at least some feedback in community orchestras. I did have to substantially rework my bowing technique in my first lesson. I showed up to my first lesson with a Brahms viola sonata I had been working on, and ended up spending two months spending a lot more time on open strings. I wasn't able to continue very long with that teacher because scheduling lessons was a huge pain, but I was able to start regular lessons with an excellent teacher several years later. I'm currently playing in a selectively auditioned community orchestra that has no repertoire limitations and performs on a 4-5 week rehearsal cycle; we played the Berlioz Symphonie Fantastique and Nielsen 2 in back-to-back concerts in February and March. I'm also currently rehearsing the Dvorak Op. 23 piano quartet in a group where both of the other string players started as adults... and none of us knew the other string players were late starters until after we formed the group! You must be registered and logged in to submit a comment. Dimitri Musafia, Master Maker of Violin and Viola Cases

title: Shelko Nets Three; Men’s Lacrosse Takes Senior Day Game v. Canisius - Manhattan University Athletics
content: RIVERDALE, NY – The Manhattan Jaspers Men's Lacrosse team (3-10, 1-6 MAAC) came away with its first conference victory of the campaign against the Golden Griffins of Canisius University (3-10, 2-5 MAAC) by a score of 9-6 inside Gaelic Park on Saturday afternoon. Head Coach Doug Sage: "We are proud of the high effort level and connectivity of our team in today's big MAAC win. Our players worked hard all week to be ready to compete for four full quarters and focused on executing at a high level in all areas of the field." "It is always special for our seniors to be able to celebrate together with their families and friends after a hard-earned win on Senior Day." The Jaspers conclude their campaign on Wednesday, April 22, when the Green and White visit the Bobcats of Quinnipiac University, beginning at 3 p.m. from the Quinnipiac Soccer and Lacrosse Stadium. The contest can be viewed for free on Quinnipiac All Access.
